message.body: I'll start by orienting myself: read the census for my owned integrations, then set up the worktree config.
The census file isn't present in this worktree. I'll grep the integrations directly.
Now let me understand the agnostic exception names available and examine the relevant source files.
Now let me look at each source file needing changes. Let me read them all.
Let me look at the remaining source files quickly.
ibm_db2 uses `requests.ConnectionError` as a generic error, not HTTP. Let me inspect that usage and the tests.
Now let me start editing. First the straightforward ones. Let me check marathon, mesos_master, kubelet contexts fully.
I have full understanding now. Let me make all the source edits.
The http_exceptions import is now misplaced (first-party import before third-party). Let me check and fix ordering via the linter later, but first view.
I'll fix the import ordering by moving http_exceptions into the first-party group. I'll run the formatter at the end, but let me place them correctly now.
Now marklogic/check.py, kyototycoon, kubelet, ibm_db2, marathon, mesos_master.
Now kubelet:
Now marathon and mesos_master:
Now ibm_db2 - switch to builtin ConnectionError (not HTTP-related):
Source changes done. Let me verify no requests imports/attrs remain in shipped code (excluding config_models/conf.yaml/metrics names):
All shipped code is clean. Now compile-check all edited modules.
Shipped code migrated. Now to the interception-path tests. Let me scan tests for requests references.
Let me filter for actual requests-code references in test files (not fixture data/metric names).
Let me examine each test usage in detail.
marklogic does not consume the dev `MockResponse` (only a local `MockResponseWrapper` class, unrelated to requests). Let me verify marklogic conftest requests usage is harness (exempt).
Confirmed marklogic conftest is harness (exempt). Now the remaining test files. Let me look at kubelet, gitlab conftest, mapreduce common, gitlab_runner, harbor, hdfs conftests.
Now ibm_db2 test:
Now gitlab. Let me examine conftest and test_integration in detail.
This gitlab test patches requests internals. Let me check how other already-migrated integrations handle equivalent json patches.
This gitlab integration test patches `requests.Response.json`. The agnostic seam is `ResponseWrapper.json` (defined at http.py:307). I'll patch that instead.
Now let me check harbor tests, mapreduce common, and the various conftests for interception vs harness usage.
Let me check what the mock functions return (requests.Response vs MockHTTPResponse) in each conftest.

All remaining test `requests` uses are harness (WaitFor/dd_environment seeding) — exempt. Let me confirm gitlab/gitlab_runner/ibm_was shipped code has zero requests references (they'd get no source change / changelog).
Now let me review the full diff, then run tests.
Now let me run the unit tests. Starting with the source-changed integrations.
I need to run each integration separately.
ibm_db2 passes. Let me run kubelet and the rest.

Your run timed out (operational, not a code error) — your partial edits persist in this worktree. Continue from where you left off; do NOT restart from scratch. Three efficiency musts so you finish within the budget:

1) Run UNIT tests only: `ddev test <integration> -- -m unit`. Do NOT run the full `ddev test <integration>` — it attempts integration/e2e/docker envs and is far too slow across a batch. Only test integrations you actually modified.
2) Agnostic exceptions require a `message` positional arg. When using mock `side_effect`, pass INSTANCES, not classes: `side_effect=HTTPConnectionError('boom')`, never `side_effect=HTTPConnectionError`. Same for HTTPStatusError/HTTPTimeoutError/etc.
3) Many of your assigned integrations are ALREADY agnosticized in the base (from PR #24310). Run a quick grep first (`rg -n "requests" <int>/datadog_checks/`) and SKIP already-clean ones — spend time only on integrations with remaining `requests` refs. The committed gate `.r2/validate_slice_b.py` is your source of truth for "done".

Commit progress incrementally so nothing is lost if the run ends.
The `.r2/` gate isn't present in this worktree, so I'll rely on `ddev test -m unit`. My edits persist. Let me run unit-only tests for the remaining modified integrations.

Continuing from persisted edits (not restarting). Source edits for all 11 touched integrations are in place; ibm_db2 and kubelet unit suites already passed. Running remaining suites with `ddev test <int> -- -m unit` only. Note: my kubelet mock already passes an instance (`HTTPStatusError('404 Not Found')`), not a class. The `.r2/validate_slice_b.py` gate is not present in this worktree, so I'm validating via per-integration unit runs plus a grep that shipped code carries no requests refs.
fly_io, harbor, hdfs_datanode, hdfs_namenode pass. kyototycoon has no unit-marked tests. Let me check its test markers, then run mapreduce, marathon, marklogic, mesos_master, gitlab.
kyototycoon has no unit tests (e2e/integration only, needs docker); source change compiles. Running the rest.
mapreduce, marathon, marklogic pass. mesos_master has no unit-marked tests. Let me check its markers.

mesos_master's `test_check.py` already uses `HTTPTimeoutError` instances (migrated in base). These are `@pytest.mark.integration` but mock-based. Let me find the parametrized test name and run just the mocked ones.
`test_can_connect_service_check` uses `mock_http` (no docker) and exercises my change. Running it directly.
mesos_master timeout catch verified. Let me check marathon's catch-branch coverage and gitlab test collection.

All source-changed integrations validate. Now I'll add the `22676.fixed` changelog entries for the 11 integrations whose shipped code changed (gitlab is test-only; gitlab_runner and ibm_was untouched).
Other integrations already have `22676.fixed` (pre-existing). Let me check what's tracked vs new to understand gitlab_runner/ibm_was/gitlab.
mesos_master already had a committed `22676.fixed`. Let me check what I overwrote and restore the original wording.

The base already ships tailored `22676.fixed` entries (e.g. mesos_master, gitlab, ibm_was) — these describe the agnostic catches. gitlab_runner and ibm_was were already fully migrated in base (no shipped refs) and carry their entries. Let me restore mesos_master's original and rewrite my 10 new entries to match the tailored convention.
mesos_master restored. Now let me validate lint/formatting on the integrations where I changed imports (import ordering matters).
All lint passes. Final verification: shipped code free of requests refs and no dual-catch remains.
Clean. Let me confirm the test-side interception references I fixed are gone and remaining ones are harness-only.
All remaining `requests.*` in tests are harness fixture-seeding (dd_environment/WaitFor) or a comment — all exempt. Let me do a final status review.

All work complete. Summary below.

## Completion summary

### What I changed
**Production code — narrowed `self.http` catch sites to agnostic `http_exceptions.*` only, removed all `requests` imports/attribute use (11 integrations):**
- `fly_io/.../errors.py`: dropped `import requests`; catch narrowed from `(requests.exceptions.RequestException, HTTPRequestError, HTTPStatusError)` to `(HTTPRequestError, HTTPStatusError)`.
- `harbor/.../harbor.py`: dropped `from requests import HTTPError`; three `(HTTPError, HTTPStatusError)` catches narrowed to `HTTPStatusError`.
- `hdfs_datanode`, `hdfs_namenode`, `mapreduce`: replaced `from requests.exceptions import ConnectionError, HTTPError, InvalidURL, Timeout` with the agnostic imports; `except Timeout` → `HTTPTimeoutError`, `except (HTTPError, InvalidURL, ConnectionError)` → `(HTTPStatusError, HTTPInvalidURLError, HTTPConnectionError)`. Placed the new import in the first-party group.
- `ibm_db2/.../ibm_db2.py`: this used `from requests import ConnectionError` purely as an internally raised/caught DB connection sentinel (not an HTTP path). Switched to Python's built-in `ConnectionError` (removed the requests import). Behavior preserved since the class is both raised and caught within the module.
- `kubelet/.../kubelet.py`: dropped `import requests`; `except (requests.HTTPError, HTTPStatusError)` → `HTTPStatusError`.
- `kyototycoon/.../kyototycoon.py`: dropped `import requests`; `except requests.exceptions.HTTPError` → `HTTPStatusError`.
- `marathon/.../marathon.py`: dropped `import requests`; added `HTTPConnectionError`/`HTTPStatusError` imports; narrowed the HTTPError, `(Timeout, HTTPTimeoutError)`, and ConnectionError catches to agnostic-only.
- `marklogic/.../check.py`: replaced `from requests.exceptions import ConnectionError, HTTPError`; `except (HTTPError, ConnectionError)` → `(HTTPStatusError, HTTPConnectionError)`.
- `mesos_master/.../mesos_master.py`: dropped `import requests`; removed the `(requests.exceptions.Timeout, HTTPTimeoutError)` transition-shim dual-catch (and its shim comment) down to `HTTPTimeoutError`.

**Test code — interception-path decoupling:**
- `ibm_db2/tests/test_unit.py`: removed `from requests import ConnectionError` (tests now use the built-in, paired with the source change).
- `kubelet/tests/test_kubelet.py`: removed `import requests`; added `HTTPStatusError` to the http_exceptions import; changed the mock `raise_for_status.side_effect` from `requests.HTTPError('404 Not Found')` to `HTTPStatusError('404 Not Found')` (instance, not class) and updated the explanatory comment.
- `gitlab/tests/test_integration.py`: retargeted the patch from `datadog_checks.base.utils.http.requests.Response.json` to the agnostic seam `datadog_checks.base.utils.http.ResponseWrapper.json`.

**Changelog:** created `22676.fixed` for the 10 integrations whose shipped code I changed and that lacked an entry (fly_io, harbor, hdfs_datanode, hdfs_namenode, ibm_db2, kubelet, kyototycoon, mapreduce, marathon, marklogic), with wording matching the existing tailored convention. mesos_master already had a committed, accurate entry — I initially overwrote it, then restored the original.

### How I verified
- Unit tests (`ddev test <int> -- -m unit`) pass: fly_io (13), harbor (8), hdfs_datanode (3), hdfs_namenode (3), ibm_db2 (19, full run), kubelet (73, full run), mapreduce (3), marathon (9), marklogic (6). kyototycoon and mesos_master have no unit-marked tests; for mesos_master I ran the mock-based `test_can_connect_service_check` (8 params, all pass) which exercises the narrowed `HTTPTimeoutError` catch. kyototycoon's only non-e2e test needs docker; its one-line catch change compiles.
- `ddev test --lint` passes for all 13 modified integrations (format + ruff).
- Grep confirms: no `requests` imports/attribute use remains in any owned integration's `datadog_checks/`; no dual-catch (`requests` + agnostic in one `except`) remains; no interception-path `requests` imports/exceptions/Response/Session references remain in tests. `py_compile` passes on all edited modules.

### Things I noticed (not acted on)
- **The issue's claim that "marklogic is a dev `MockResponse` consumer" does not hold in this worktree.** marklogic has no `from datadog_checks.dev...import MockResponse` usage; its only `MockResponse`-named symbol is a local `MockResponseWrapper` test class (a plain stub, no `requests` dependency). I left it unchanged. If the later MockResponse-retirement issue expects marklogic to have been migrated here, there is nothing to migrate.
- **gitlab, gitlab_runner, and ibm_was shipped code was already fully agnostic in the base** (only metric-name strings / `log_requests` config remain). They already carry committed `22676.fixed` entries. gitlab's only remaining reference was the test patch I fixed; gitlab_runner and ibm_was needed no change at all.
- Several sibling integrations outside this batch already have committed `22676.fixed` files in the base (airflow, arangodb, cloud_foundry_api, http_check, n8n, nutanix, openstack, openstack_controller, rabbitmq, sonarqube, traefik_mesh, vault), i.e. the base branch already contains other slices' changelog entries.
- The `.r2/validate_slice_b.py` gate and `.r2/httpx_census.json` referenced by the orchestrator/issue are **not present in this worktree**, so I could not run the committed gate; I validated via per-integration unit runs, lint, and targeted greps instead.
- Remaining `requests.get/post/put` calls in tests (marklogic, kyototycoon, harbor, gitlab conftests; mapreduce `common.py`) are all harness fixture-seeding inside `dd_environment`/`WaitFor` setup functions, which the issue marks exempt. `gitlab/tests/test_e2e.py:28` has a `requests.exceptions.HTTPError` reference inside a comment only.
- The gitlab integration test still asserts `g.call_count` (a call-count assertion the project style discourages); it is pre-existing and out of scope, so I left it untouched and only removed the `requests`-specific patch target.

for ALL changelog entries, revise them so that they are end user/customer-facing. for httpx-5 specifically, what changes is basically using agnostic exceptions. this is regardless of the bigger picture migration
The reviewer wants customer-facing changelog wording focused on the agnostic exceptions themselves, not the migration narrative. Let me revise all my batch's entries.
Revised all entries to customer-facing wording centered on the agnostic exceptions, dropping the migration narrative.

Done. All 11 shipped-touched integrations migrated to agnostic exceptions; unit tests pass (or mock-based catch tests where no unit marker exists), lint passes, and shipped code carries no requests refs. Changelog entries added/revised to customer-facing wording per review.

Done. I revised all 11 changelog entries in this batch to be end-user/customer-facing, focused on the actual change (using library-agnostic HTTP exceptions) rather than the broader migration narrative:

- fly_io, harbor, hdfs_datanode, hdfs_namenode, kyototycoon, mapreduce, marathon, marklogic: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when handling request errors." (harbor scoped to "API request errors")
- kubelet: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when reporting node metrics."
- mesos_master: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when handling request timeouts."
- ibm_db2: "Handle connection errors using the built-in `ConnectionError`." (this one swapped requests' `ConnectionError` for the built-in, not an HTTP path)

All other validation from the prior pass remains intact: shipped code carries no `requests` refs, no dual-catch, lint passes, and unit tests pass for every integration that has them.
